the_one_thing.statement: The equal sign is relational, so a multiplication equation can be written with the product on either side — `a × b = c` and `c = a × b` are equivalent forms of the same fact, and neither is preferred.
the_one_thing.success_indicator: After M02, the student (a) reads both `4 × 5 = 20` and `20 = 4 × 5` as correct, equivalent names for the same rectangle; (b) produces the reversed-orientation form when given the standard form (and vice versa) for any fact; (c) explains that the equal sign means both sides have the same value, not "write the answer here."
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: If Mode C is not enforced in M03+ post-Check displays, M02's remediation work for U1.6 is undone. Both-orientation post-Check display from M03 onward must be a hard engineering constraint (RISK-M02-1).

student_facing_goal: You will see the rectangle you built in the last lesson and write the same fact two ways — `4 × 5 = 20` and `20 = 4 × 5` — and learn that neither way is preferred, because the equal sign means both sides have the same value.
exit_check_tests: Given a rectangle (5 × 8 = 40), select ALL correct equation forms — both orientations (recognition — EC.1 item EC1)
Given a standard-orientation equation (`7 × 6 = 42`), write the reversed-orientation form (production — EC.1 item EC2)
Given a reversed-orientation equation (`36 = 9 × 4`), write the standard-orientation form (production — EC.1 item EC3)

required_phrases: Required Phrases (Concrete + Relational sections — Beats 1–4, W.1 through L.3):*

forbidden_phrases.phrase: (Engineering-internal zone — listed here for content author reference.)
